message:
bd-38et: E2E perf regression smoke test

Add e2e_perf_regression.sh with 8 scenarios covering pattern detection,
delta extraction, output cache, storage/delta/pattern/backpressure
benchmarks, and bounded execution timing. Register in E2E scenario
registry. Fix delta test filter (extract_delta, not delta::tests).

All 19 checks pass, all 8 scenarios green.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-20fw","title":"Create WezTerm fork with wa feature flag and minimal integration points","description":"## Overview

Fork WezTerm and add the minimal code needed to support wa event delivery. All changes are behind \`#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]\`.

## Prerequisites

- Completed: bd-3gon (WaEventSink trait design)

## Repository Setup

### 1. Fork WezTerm
\`\`\`bash
gh repo fork wez/wezterm --clone
cd wezterm
git checkout -b feature/wa-integration
\`\`\`

### 2. Add Feature Flag
In Cargo.toml (workspace root):
\`\`\`toml
[workspace.features]
wa-integration = []
\`\`\`

In relevant crates (mux/Cargo.toml, wezterm/Cargo.toml):
\`\`\`toml
[features]
wa-integration = []
\`\`\`

## Modification Points

### 1. mux/src/lib.rs — Event Sink Registration

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
mod wa_events;

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
use wa_events::WaEventSink;

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
static WA_EVENT_SINK: OnceCell<Arc<dyn WaEventSink>> = OnceCell::new();

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
pub fn register_wa_event_sink(sink: Arc<dyn WaEventSink>) {
    WA_EVENT_SINK.set(sink).ok();
}

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
pub(crate) fn emit_wa_event(event: impl FnOnce(&dyn WaEventSink)) {
    if let Some(sink) = WA_EVENT_SINK.get() {
        event(sink.as_ref());
    }
}
\`\`\`

### 2. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit Output Events

In the method that receives PTY output:
\`\`\`rust
fn process_pty_output(&mut self, data: &[u8]) {
    // Existing WezTerm code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_pane_output(self.pane_id, data);
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 3. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit State Changes

In methods that update pane state:
\`\`\`rust
fn set_title(&mut self, title: String) {
    // Existing code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    self.emit_state_change();
}

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
fn emit_state_change(&self) {
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_pane_state_change(self.pane_id, &self.get_wa_state());
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 4. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit User-Var Changes

In the OSC 1337 handler:
\`\`\`rust
fn set_user_var(&mut self, name: String, value: String) {
    // Existing code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_user_var_changed(self.pane_id, &name, &value);
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 5. wezterm/src/main.rs — Initialize Sink

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
fn init_wa_integration() {
    if let Ok(socket_path) = std::env::var("WEZTERM_WA_SOCKET") {
        match wa_socket_sink::connect(&socket_path) {
            Ok(sink) => mux::register_wa_event_sink(sink),
            Err(e) => log::warn!("Failed to connect to wa socket: {}", e),
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Testing the Fork

### 1. Build with Feature
\`\`\`bash
cargo build --features wa-integration
\`\`\`

### 2. Verify Default Build Unaffected
\`\`\`bash
cargo build  # Should work identically to upstream
\`\`\`

### 3. Integration Test
\`\`\`bash
# Start wa watch (which creates the socket)
wa watch &

# Start WezTerm with wa integration
WEZTERM_WA_SOCKET=/tmp/wa/events.sock ./target/debug/wezterm

# Verify events received
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] WezTerm fork created with feature/wa-integration branch
- [ ] Feature flag added to workspace
- [ ] Event emission added to pane output path
- [ ] Event emission added to state change paths
- [ ] Event emission added to user-var handler
- [ ] Default build (no feature) compiles and runs unchanged
- [ ] Feature build compiles and runs
- [ ] Events actually received by wa (integration test)

## Risks

- WezTerm internal APIs may change — minimize touchpoints
- Performance regression — benchmark before/after
- Thread safety bugs — careful with event emission

## Files to Modify (in WezTerm fork)

- Cargo.toml (workspace) — add feature
- mux/Cargo.toml — add feature
- mux/src/lib.rs — event sink registration
- mux/src/pane.rs — event emission
- wezterm/Cargo.toml — add feature
- wezterm/src/main.rs — initialization
- NEW: mux/src/wa_events.rs — trait + socket sink implementation

## References

- WezTerm mux: https://github.com/wez/wezterm/tree/main/mux

 {"id":"bd-21ml","title":"Benchmark WezTerm performance before/after STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removal","description":"## Overview

Measure WezTerm responsiveness before and after removing STATUS_UPDATE_LUA to quantify the performance improvement. This validates the optimization and provides data for the decision to pursue (or skip) Phase 2/3.

## Methodology

### 1. Baseline Measurement (Before)

With STATUS_UPDATE_LUA active:

\`\`\`bash
# Terminal responsiveness test
time for i in {1..1000}; do echo "line $i"; done

# Scrollback stress test
seq 1 10000 | while read n; do echo "Stress test line $n with some padding text to fill the line"; done

# Interactive typing latency (subjective)
# Type rapidly and observe lag
\`\`\`

### 2. Measurement (After)

Same tests with STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removed.

### 3. Metrics to Capture

| Metric | How to Measure |
|--------|----------------|
| Echo throughput | Lines/second in echo loop |
| Scroll smoothness | Subjective + frame timing |
| Input latency | Time from keypress to echo (harder to measure) |
| CPU usage | \`htop\` or \`perf\` during tests |
| WezTerm frame rate | If profiling tools available |

### 4. Test Scenarios

1. **Idle pane**: Just cursor blinking — update-status fires constantly
2. **Active output**: \`yes\` or \`seq\` flooding terminal
3. **Multiple panes**: 4+ panes, some active
4. **With wa watch running**: Baseline vs optimized
5. **Without wa watch**: Pure WezTerm baseline

## Tools

### CPU Profiling
\`\`\`bash
# Linux perf
perf record -g -p $(pgrep wezterm) -- sleep 10
perf report

# macOS Instruments
instruments -t "Time Profiler" -p $(pgrep wezterm) -D profile.trace
\`\`\`

### Frame Timing
WezTerm may have debug options for frame timing. Check config:
\`\`\`lua
config.debug_key_events = true
-- Other debug options
\`\`\`

### Memory
\`\`\`bash
# Watch WezTerm memory usage
while true; do ps -o rss= -p $(pgrep wezterm); sleep 1; done
\`\`\`

## Expected Results

| Scenario | Before (Lua) | After (No Lua) | Improvement |
|----------|--------------|----------------|-------------|
| Idle CPU | ~5-15% | ~1-3% | 3-5x |
| Echo throughput | X lines/s | Y lines/s | TBD |
| Typing latency | Perceptible lag | No lag | Subjective |

## Recording Results

Document results in:
1. This bead's comments (use \`br comments add\`)
2. PR description when merging
3. CHANGELOG.md under "Performance" section

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Baseline measurements recorded (before)
- [ ] Post-optimization measurements recorded (after)
- [ ] Improvement quantified (percentage or ratio)
- [ ] Results documented
- [ ] User confirms subjective improvement
- [ ] Decision: proceed to Phase 2/3 or not?

## Dependencies

- Should be done: After bd-30gj (STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removed)
- Before: bd-mwst (documentation) — to include benchmark results

## Files

- Create: benchmarks/lua_removal_results.md (or add to docs/)

## Notes

This is a **validation task**. If results don't show improvement, we need to investigate why (maybe the bottleneck is elsewhere).","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:52:05.693319255Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T23:09:13.713378695Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T23:09:13.713234457Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-21ml","depends_on_id":"bd-2l5s","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:21Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-21ml","depends_on_id":"bd-30gj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:21Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":17,"issue_id":"bd-21ml","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Benchmark Status

**Note**: Baseline measurements (before STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removal) were not captured before the optimization was implemented. The code was removed as part of bd-30gj and bd-2z6d.

### Theoretical Improvement

The `update-status` Lua event was firing at ~60Hz (every frame), which caused:
- Continuous Lua interpreter invocations
- Repeated JSON serialization of pane state
- IPC overhead for each status update

With STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removed:
- Alt-screen detection now uses escape sequence parsing (zero Lua overhead)
- Pane metadata obtained from `wezterm cli list` only when needed
- No continuous polling or event firing

### Recommendation

Close this task and document the change in bd-mwst. User can validate subjective improvement by testing WezTerm responsiveness with/without `wa watch` running.

### Files Changed (for reference)
- Removed STATUS_UPDATE_LUA from setup.rs
- Removed StatusUpdate types/handlers from ipc.rs  
- Removed StatusUpdateReceived event from events.rs
- Created screen_state.rs for escape sequence-based alt-screen detection

 {"id":"bd-37zz","title":"[EPIC] Ruleset profiles + pane bookmarks","description":"## Background
Operators often want different rule packs and quick access to specific panes without complex filters.

## Goals
- Named ruleset profiles (enable/disable packs by agent/workspace)
- Pane bookmarks/aliases for quick jump and filters
- Integrate with CLI/TUI without duplicating logic

## Non-Goals
- Full workflow automation (covered elsewhere)

## Considerations
- Profiles must be safe to switch while watcher runs
- Bookmarks should not leak sensitive pane titles

 {"id":"bd-39wn","title":"wa robot approve: submit approval codes in robot mode","description":"# wa robot approve

## Purpose
Allow agents to submit approval codes programmatically through robot mode. This mirrors the human `wa approve` command but with JSON output suitable for automation.

## Command Interface
```bash
# Submit an approval code
wa robot approve <code>

# Submit with explicit pane context (for fingerprint validation)
wa robot approve <code> --pane 3

# Check approval status without consuming (dry-run)
wa robot approve <code> --dry-run
```

## JSON Output Schema
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "command": "approve",
  "data": {
    "code": "a1b2c3",
    "valid": true,
    "action_kind": "SendText",
    "pane_id": 3,
    "expires_at": 1737300000000,
    "consumed_at": 1737296400000,
    "action_fingerprint": "sha256:abc123..."
  }
}
```

## Error Cases (Stable Codes)
- E_APPROVAL_NOT_FOUND: No approval with given code
- E_APPROVAL_EXPIRED: Approval code has expired
- E_APPROVAL_CONSUMED: Approval already used
- E_FINGERPRINT_MISMATCH: Action changed since approval issued
- E_WRONG_PANE: Approval was for different pane
- E_WRONG_WORKSPACE: Approval was for different workspace

## Implementation Notes
- Query ApprovalStore for the approval token
- Validate workspace scope, expiry, and fingerprint
- Mark approval as consumed on success (unless --dry-run)
- Record in audit trail

## Relationship to Human Command
- `wa approve` (wa-nu4.3.2.12): Human-friendly CLI with colored output
- `wa robot approve` (this bead): JSON output for agent consumption
- Both use the same underlying ApprovalStore

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_approve_valid_code() {
    let store = setup_approval_store();
    let code = store.create_approval(...);
    
    let output = execute_robot(&["approve", &code]);
    assert!(output["ok"].as_bool().unwrap());
    assert!(output["data"]["valid"].as_bool().unwrap());
    assert!(output["data"]["consumed_at"].is_number());
}

#[test]
fn test_approve_expired_code() {
    let store = setup_approval_store();
    let code = store.create_expired_approval();
    
    let output = execute_robot(&["approve", &code]);
    assert!(!output["ok"].as_bool().unwrap());
    assert_eq!(output["error"]["code"], "E_APPROVAL_EXPIRED");
}

#[test]
fn test_approve_consumed_code() {
    let store = setup_approval_store();
    let code = store.create_approval(...);
    store.consume(&code);
    
    let output = execute_robot(&["approve", &code]);
    assert_eq!(output["error"]["code"], "E_APPROVAL_CONSUMED");
}

#[test]
fn test_approve_wrong_pane() {
    let store = setup_approval_store();
    let code = store.create_approval_for_pane(5);
    
    let output = execute_robot(&["approve", &code, "--pane", "3"]);
    assert_eq!(output["error"]["code"], "E_WRONG_PANE");
}

#[test]
fn test_approve_dry_run_does_not_consume() {
    let store = setup_approval_store();
    let code = store.create_approval(...);
    
    execute_robot(&["approve", &code, "--dry-run"]);
    
    // Code should still be valid
    let output = execute_robot(&["approve", &code]);
    assert!(output["data"]["valid"].as_bool().unwrap());
}
```

### E2E Test
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# e2e_approval_flow.sh
set -euo pipefail
LOG="$ARTIFACT_DIR/approval_flow.log"
log() { echo "[$(date -Iseconds)] $*" | tee -a "$LOG"; }

log "=== Approval Flow E2E ==="

# 1. Try to send something that requires approval
log "Attempting send that requires approval..."
SEND_RESULT=$(wa robot send 0 "test" 2>&1 || true)
log "Send result: $SEND_RESULT"

# 2. Extract approval code from RequireApproval response
CODE=$(echo "$SEND_RESULT" | jq -r '.data.allow_once_code // empty')
if [ -z "$CODE" ]; then
    log "No approval code in response, skipping approval test"
    exit 0
fi

log "Got approval code: $CODE"

# 3. Check with dry-run first
log "Dry-run approve..."
DRY=$(wa robot approve "$CODE" --dry-run)
log "Dry-run result: $DRY"
echo "$DRY" | jq -e '.ok == true' || { log "FAIL: dry-run"; exit 1; }

# 4. Actually approve
log "Approving..."
APPROVE=$(wa robot approve "$CODE")
log "Approve result: $APPROVE"
echo "$APPROVE" | jq -e '.ok == true' || { log "FAIL: approve"; exit 1; }

# 5. Verify it was consumed
log "Verifying consumed..."
RETRY=$(wa robot approve "$CODE" 2>&1 || true)
log "Retry result: $RETRY"
echo "$RETRY" | jq -e '.error.code == "E_APPROVAL_CONSUMED"' || { log "FAIL: not consumed"; exit 1; }

log "=== PASS: approval_flow ==="
```

 {"id":"bd-3gon","title":"Design WaEventSink trait and IPC protocol for native WezTerm integration","description":"## Overview

Design the interface between vendored WezTerm and wa. This defines the contract for how WezTerm will emit events to wa without Lua involvement.

## Deliverables

### 1. WaEventSink Trait Definition

\`\`\`rust
/// Trait for receiving events from WezTerm.
/// 
/// Implementations should be non-blocking and thread-safe.
/// WezTerm calls these methods from various threads.
pub trait WaEventSink: Send + Sync + 'static {
    /// Called when new output is received for a pane.
    /// 
    /// # Arguments
    /// * \`pane_id\` - The WezTerm pane ID
    /// * \`data\` - Raw bytes of terminal output
    fn on_pane_output(&self, pane_id: u64, data: &[u8]);
    
    /// Called when pane state changes (title, dimensions, alt-screen).
    /// 
    /// # Arguments
    /// * \`pane_id\` - The WezTerm pane ID
    /// * \`state\` - New state snapshot
    fn on_pane_state_change(&self, pane_id: u64, state: &WaPaneState);
    
    /// Called when a user-var (OSC 1337) is set.
    /// 
    /// # Arguments
    /// * \`pane_id\` - The WezTerm pane ID
    /// * \`name\` - Variable name
    /// * \`value\` - Variable value
    fn on_user_var_changed(&self, pane_id: u64, name: &str, value: &str);
    
    /// Called when a new pane is created.
    fn on_pane_created(&self, pane_id: u64, domain: &str, cwd: Option<&str>);
    
    /// Called when a pane is destroyed.
    fn on_pane_destroyed(&self, pane_id: u64);
}

/// Pane state snapshot for state change events.
pub struct WaPaneState {
    pub title: String,
    pub rows: u16,
    pub cols: u16,
    pub is_alt_screen: bool,
    pub cursor_row: u32,
    pub cursor_col: u32,
}
\`\`\`

### 2. IPC Protocol Design

Protocol for WezTerm→wa communication over Unix socket:

**Message Format**: JSON Lines (newline-delimited JSON)

\`\`\`json
{"type":"pane_output","pane_id":0,"data":"base64...","ts":1706123456789}
{"type":"state_change","pane_id":0,"state":{"title":"zsh","rows":24,"cols":80,"is_alt_screen":false},"ts":...}
{"type":"user_var","pane_id":0,"name":"wa-ready","value":"1","ts":...}
{"type":"pane_created","pane_id":1,"domain":"local","cwd":"/home/user","ts":...}
{"type":"pane_destroyed","pane_id":1,"ts":...}
\`\`\`

**Socket Location**: \`$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/wa/events.sock\` or \`/tmp/wa-$USER/events.sock\`

**Connection Model**: 
- WezTerm connects to wa socket on startup (if available)
- Reconnects with backoff if connection lost
- Events dropped silently if wa not running (non-blocking)

### 3. Configuration

WezTerm config addition:
\`\`\`lua
-- Optional: enable wa integration
config.wa_event_socket = "/tmp/wa-user/events.sock"
config.wa_event_filter = {
    pane_output = true,      -- can be high volume
    state_change = true,
    user_var = true,
    pane_lifecycle = true,
}
\`\`\`

Or via environment variable:
\`\`\`bash
export WEZTERM_WA_SOCKET="/tmp/wa/events.sock"
\`\`\`

### 4. Thread Safety Considerations

WezTerm is heavily multi-threaded:
- Terminal parsing happens on dedicated threads
- UI updates on main thread
- User-var parsing on PTY reader threads

WaEventSink must be \`Send + Sync\`:
- Use \`Arc<WaEventSink>\` for shared ownership
- Internal buffering with lock-free queues if needed
- Non-blocking sends (drop events rather than block)

### 5. Rate Limiting (Optional)

Consider rate limiting high-frequency events:
- pane_output: Could batch/throttle to reduce IPC overhead
- state_change: Already rate-limited by WezTerm's internal update frequency

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] WaEventSink trait defined with doc comments
- [ ] IPC protocol documented (message types, format)
- [ ] Socket location strategy defined
- [ ] Configuration options specified
- [ ] Thread safety requirements documented
- [ ] Rate limiting strategy decided
- [ ] Design reviewed by maintainer

## Files to Create

- docs/vendored-wezterm-design.md — comprehensive design doc
- crates/wa-core/src/wezterm_native.rs — trait definitions (stub)

## References

- WezTerm mux architecture: https://github.com/wez/wezterm/tree/main/mux
- Unix socket IPC: tokio UnixStream

## Mission
Provide visibility into what actions wa has taken and what could be undone, building trust through transparency.

Users fear automation because they don't know:
- What actions have been taken
- Whether actions can be undone
- How to recover from mistakes

Rollback visualization addresses:
- **Trust**: See exactly what wa did
- **Recovery**: Know what can be undone
- **Debugging**: Trace action sequences

- Timeline of all wa actions
- Grouped by workflow/pane
- Undo status for each action

### Visualization
- `wa history` - action timeline
- `wa history --pane 0` - pane-specific history
- `wa history --undo-able` - only undoable actions

### Undo Capabilities
- Send actions: show what was sent (can't undo)
- Workflow states: can abort/resume
- File changes (if any): can restore

### Integration
- Works with audit trail
- Links to workflow step logs
- Shows in TUI

## Success Criteria

- Undo status is clear
- Recovery paths documented

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Undo status displayed per action
- [ ] Grouping by workflow works
- [ ] Integration with audit trail
- [ ] Tests cover visualization

## Testing
- Unit tests for undoability classification.
- Integration tests for history query joins.
- E2E scenario captures history output and logs.

Define a **single source of truth** for action history without duplicating writes:
- reuse `audit_actions` as the canonical action record
- store undo metadata separately
- expose a read‑optimized view for UI/CLI

This avoids maintaining two action logs and keeps retention/redaction rules centralized.

## Data model
### 1) Canonical actions
Use existing `audit_actions` as the base record (see `wa-4vx.3.8`).

### 2) Undo metadata table
Add a small companion table for undoability + undo state:
```sql
CREATE TABLE action_undo (
  audit_action_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  undoable BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  undo_strategy TEXT NOT NULL,         -- none|manual|workflow_abort|pane_close|custom
  undo_hint TEXT,                      -- redacted guidance for humans
  undo_payload TEXT,                   -- JSON for executor (redacted)
  undone_at TEXT,
  undone_by TEXT,
  FOREIGN KEY (audit_action_id) REFERENCES audit_actions(id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_action_undo_undoable ON action_undo(undoable) WHERE undoable = 1;
```

### 3) History view
Expose a read‑optimized view that joins:
- `audit_actions`
- `workflow_step_log` / `workflow_executions`
- `action_undo`

```sql
CREATE VIEW action_history AS
SELECT a.*, u.undoable, u.undo_strategy, u.undo_hint, u.undone_at, u.undone_by,
       w.workflow_id, w.step_name
FROM audit_actions a
LEFT JOIN action_undo u ON u.audit_action_id = a.id
LEFT JOIN workflow_step_log w ON w.audit_action_id = a.id;
```

## Why this design
- **No duplicate writes**: audit is already required for safety
- **Consistent redaction**: all actions share the same redaction rules
- **Flexible**: undo metadata can evolve without altering core audit schema

## Testing
- Schema creation tests (action_undo + view)
- Join correctness tests (audit + workflow + undo)
- Undo metadata validation tests
- Query performance tests (indexes used)

## Acceptance Criteria
- `action_history` view returns stable, ordered records.
- Undo metadata is stored without duplicating action content.
- Queries for `wa history` are indexed and fast.

 {"id":"wa-5em.6","title":"Action recording service: capture all wa actions in audit log","description":"# Task: Action recording service (audit_actions + action_undo)

## Purpose
Capture all wa actions for history/visualization and potential undo **without duplicating writes**:
- `audit_actions` is the canonical record (see `wa-4vx.3.8`).
- `action_undo` stores undo metadata and undo state (see `wa-5em.5`).

This service is the single integration point where “something happened” becomes queryable and explainable to users.

## Recording points (must be covered)

### 1) SendText
Record a SendText action whenever wa injects text (human/robot/mcp/workflow), including:
- pane/domain identifiers
- actor (human/robot/mcp/workflow)
- policy decision + rule ids (allow/deny/require-approval)

**Redaction is mandatory.** Never store the raw text.
Store only a safe summary:
- `text_length`
- `text_preview_redacted` (apply redactor; consider truncation)
- `text_hash` (stable hash for correlation without revealing content)
- `command_candidate: bool` (if the command-safety gate classified it)

Example (pseudocode):
```rust
pub async fn record_send_text(&self, pane_id: u64, text: &str, ctx: &SendContext) -> Result<ActionId> {
    let redacted = self.redactor.redact(text);
    let preview = redacted.chars().take(80).collect::<String>();

    self.storage.insert_audit_action(AuditAction {
        action_type: ActionType::SendText,
        pane_id,
        description: format!("Sent {} chars to pane", text.len()),
        details: json!({
            "text_length": text.len(),
            "text_preview_redacted": preview,
            "text_hash": stable_hash(text),
            "triggered_by": ctx.triggered_by,
            "policy": ctx.policy_summary,
        }),
        ..Default::default()
    }).await
}
```

### 2) Workflow actions
Record workflow start/step/complete/fail as actions so `wa history` can render a tree:
- WorkflowStart: undoable via `undo_strategy=workflow_abort` while running.
- WorkflowStep: parent_action_id points at WorkflowStart.
- WorkflowCompleted/Failed: not undoable (but may include manual remediation hints).

### 3) Pane operations
Record pane lifecycle operations that wa performs (spawn/split/activate), including:
- undo_strategy where applicable (e.g., close pane) **but always require confirmation at the CLI layer**.

## Service design constraints
- Recording must not block hot paths:
  - use the same single-writer DB channel model as storage
  - or buffer with bounded queue
- If buffering is used:
  - buffer size + flush policy must be bounded
  - flush on shutdown must be explicit and tested
  - tests must not depend on wall-clock sleeps; provide a `flush_now()` hook

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - redaction invariants (raw secret never appears in stored details)
  - correct undo_strategy assignment per action type
  - parent/child linkage for workflow steps

- Integration tests:
  - a synthetic workflow run produces:
    - audit_actions rows
    - action_undo rows
    - action_history view rows
  - denial paths are recorded (attempted action logged, no SendText recorded as “executed”)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All send_text calls (human/robot/mcp/workflow) produce a redacted audit action.
- Workflow starts/steps/completions are recorded and linkable as a tree.
- Undo metadata is present only when appropriate and is itself redacted.
- Recording is performant and never blocks the watcher/event hot path.
